mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Indonesia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the south-east. It sits on the equator between mainland Asia and Australia 🌏

mapQuestions.question: How many islands does Indonesia have?
mapQuestions.answer: Over 17,000 islands. Only about 6,000 of them are inhabited, the rest are empty.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Indonesia?
mapQuestions.answer: Jakarta, on the island of Java. It is one of the biggest cities in the world, with over 30 million people in the wider area. (They are building a brand new capital city called Nusantara on Borneo.)

mapQuestions.question: Which big country is just south of Indonesia?
mapQuestions.answer: Australia. From northern Australia you can almost see Indonesia across the Timor Sea.

mapQuestions.question: What is the most active volcano in Indonesia?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Merapi on Java, which erupts every few years. Indonesia sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire, so it has around 130 active volcanoes, more than any other country.

foods.name: Nasi goreng
foods.description: Fried rice with sweet soy sauce (kecap manis), egg on top, crispy shallots, and usually chicken or prawns. The unofficial national dish.

foods.name: Sate
foods.description: Small skewers of chicken, beef or goat grilled over charcoal, served with thick peanut sauce. The smell from a sate cart at dusk is unforgettable.

foods.name: Gado-gado
foods.description: A mix of steamed vegetables, boiled egg, tofu and tempeh, drenched in peanut sauce. "Gado-gado" literally means "mix-mix".

foods.name: Rendang
foods.description: Beef slow-cooked for hours in coconut milk and spices until it is dark, dry and unbelievably rich. Once voted the most delicious dish in the world.

foods.name: Mie goreng
foods.description: Fried noodles with sweet soy, chilli, vegetables, and usually a fried egg on top. Same family as nasi goreng but with noodles.

foods.name: Martabak manis
foods.description: A thick sweet pancake folded over chocolate, condensed milk, peanuts and grated cheese. Sounds chaotic, tastes incredible.

scavengerItems.label: A whole family riding on one scooter (it really happens)
A small basket of flower offerings on a doorstep or pavement (canang sari)
Someone selling fresh coconuts with a straw
A becak (a bicycle with a passenger seat in front)
A small gecko on a wall (and listen for its loud chirp at night)
A bowl of sambal chilli sauce on a table
A long-tailed macaque trying to steal something
A huge kite flying high in the sky (Indonesia loves kites)
A stone temple gate (a "candi bentar" split gate) in Bali
A wayang (shadow puppet) or a traditional mask in a shop
A volcano in the distance (Indonesia has 130 active ones)

geoMatches.description: Small island, huge personality. Surf beaches in the south, volcanoes and rice terraces in the centre, temples on every corner.
The mega-capital on Java. Skyscrapers, traffic jams that can last hours, and some of the best street food in the country.
The cultural heart of Java. Home to a sultan, batik workshops, and the gigantic 1,200-year-old Buddhist temple of Borobudur.
Dry, golden islands where Komodo dragons (the biggest lizards on earth) roam. You can only visit with a ranger holding a forked stick.
A huge wild island in the west. Jungles with orangutans and tigers, lakes inside ancient volcanoes, and the spiciest food in Indonesia.
Hundreds of jungle-covered islands in the far east. The most biodiverse coral reefs anywhere on the planet, full of reef sharks and turtles.

senses.smell: Frangipani flowers, clove cigarettes (kretek), sate smoke, durian fruit, jasmine incense from temple offerings, rain on hot tarmac…
senses.hear: Gamelan music drifting from a courtyard, scooter horns, the call to prayer, geckos chirping at night, roosters at 4am whether you like it or not…
senses.taste: Spicy sambal, sweet kecap manis, salty crispy shallots, cooling coconut water, sticky palm sugar, the punch of a fresh chilli…
senses.feel: Sticky equatorial heat, cool tile floors in a homestay, soft volcanic sand, warm sea water year round, the splash of a sudden afternoon downpour…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are there little flower baskets everywhere?
stories.body: In Bali you will see tiny woven palm-leaf baskets called "canang sari" on doorsteps, pavements, dashboards, even on top of statues. Every single one has been freshly made and offered that morning by a Balinese Hindu, usually a woman of the household. Inside there are flowers (one colour for each direction), a few grains of rice, sometimes a sweet or a cigarette, and a stick of incense. They are offerings to the gods, made three times a day. A typical family makes dozens every day, and the whole island is doing it at the same time.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you had to make a small offering every morning, what would you put in it, and who would it be for?

stories.question: What is the biggest Buddhist temple in the world?
stories.body: Borobudur sits in the middle of Java and is the largest Buddhist monument on earth. It was built around the year 800, more than 1,200 years ago, by hand, with over 2 million volcanic stones. Then, around 1,000 years ago, the people of the region converted to Islam, and Borobudur was abandoned. The jungle slowly swallowed it. It stayed hidden for hundreds of years until it was rediscovered in 1814. Now it is a UNESCO site, and pilgrims walk clockwise up its nine levels at sunrise to reach the top.

stories.question: Are Komodo dragons actually dangerous?
stories.body: Yes. Komodo dragons grow up to 3 metres long and can weigh more than 70kg. They are fast, they can climb, they can swim between islands, and their saliva is full of bacteria so deadly that one bite can take down a water buffalo within a few days. They live only on a handful of Indonesian islands, nowhere else on earth. When you visit, a park ranger walks with you holding a long forked stick to gently push dragons away if they come too close. They do, sometimes.

stories.question: Why is Indonesia building a brand new capital?
stories.body: Jakarta is sinking. The city sits on a swampy delta and has been pumping so much groundwater out from below that whole districts have dropped several metres in the last 30 years. Parts of it now flood at high tide. So in 2019 the government decided to move the capital to a new city called Nusantara, deep in the jungle on the island of Borneo, hundreds of kilometres away. They are building it from scratch, planning for a million people. It is one of the most ambitious city-building projects of our lifetime.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you had to design a brand new capital city from nothing, what is the first thing you would build?

stories.question: What is a wayang shadow puppet show?
stories.body: On Java and Bali, people have been telling stories with shadow puppets for over a thousand years. The puppets are flat, made of buffalo leather, beautifully painted, with long sticks for arms. A puppet master (called a "dalang") sits behind a white cotton screen with a lamp shining through, and moves the puppets so the audience on the other side sees their shadows dancing. The shows can last all night, sometimes from sunset to sunrise, telling huge old stories about gods and battles. A live gamelan orchestra clangs and chimes along beside the dalang the whole time.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could put on your own shadow puppet show, what story would you tell?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Indonesia so far?
What's the spiciest thing you've tried? Out of 10, how brave were you?
What's one thing that surprises you about how Indonesian families live?
Have you found a favourite island or place so far? Why that one?
If you could bring one Indonesian habit home with you, what would it be?
Indonesia has hundreds of different ethnic groups and languages. What do you think holds it all together as one country?

wordSearch.hint: Indonesian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Long-tailed macaque
animals.description: In Ubud's Monkey Forest in Bali, in temples, and on roadsides everywhere.

animals.name: House gecko
animals.description: On every wall, every ceiling, every restaurant. Their voice sounds like "geck-o".

animals.name: Bali cow (Banteng)
animals.description: Working cattle ploughing Bali's rice paddies — descendants of the wild banteng.

animals.name: Reticulated python
animals.description: The longest snake in the world lives here. (Usually only in rural areas, mostly hidden!)

animals.name: Komodo dragon
animals.description: The world's biggest lizard — up to 3m long. Only on Komodo, Rinca and a few small islands.

animals.name: Sumatran orangutan
animals.description: Critically endangered, in Gunung Leuser National Park in northern Sumatra.
